content:
# Acts Chapter 9 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 235-237 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_009_page_001.jpg, ACT_009_page_002.jpg, ACT_009_page_003.jpg

---

**1** But Saul, still breathing threat and murder against the disciples of the Lord, went to the high priest **2** and asked from him letters to Damascus, to the synagogues, so that if he found any who were of the Way, both men and women, he might bring them bound to Jerusalem. **3** And as he journeyed, it came to pass that he drew near to Damascus, and suddenly a light from heaven flashed around him. **4** And falling to the ground, he heard a voice saying to him, "Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?" **5** And he said, "Who are you, Lord?" And he said, "I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting. **6** But rise and enter into the city, and it shall be told to you what you must do." **7** And the men who were journeying with him stood speechless, hearing indeed the voice but seeing no one. **8** And Saul arose from the ground, and though his eyes were opened, he saw nothing. And leading him by the hand, they brought him into Damascus. **9** And he was three days without sight, and neither ate nor drank.

**10** Now there was a certain disciple in Damascus named Ananias, and the Lord said to him in a vision, "Ananias." And he said, "Behold, I am here, Lord." **11** And the Lord said to him, "Rise and go to the street called Straight, and inquire at the house of Judas for one named Saul, of Tarsus; for behold, he is praying, **12** and he has seen in a vision a man named Ananias coming in and laying hands on him, so that he might receive his sight." **13** But Ananias answered, "Lord, I have heard from many concerning this man, how much evil he has done to your saints in Jerusalem. **14** And here he has authority from the chief priests to bind all who call upon your name." **15** But the Lord said to him, "Go, for this one is a chosen vessel to me, to bear my name before the Gentiles and kings and the sons of Israel. **16** For I will show him how much he must suffer for the sake of my name."

**17** And Ananias departed and entered the house, and laying his hands upon him, he said, "Brother Saul, the Lord has sent me -- Jesus, who appeared to you on the road by which you were coming -- so that you may receive your sight and be filled with the Holy Spirit." **18** And immediately there fell from his eyes something like scales, and he received his sight. And he rose and was baptized, **19** and having taken food, he was strengthened.

And he was with the disciples in Damascus for some days. **20** And immediately in the synagogues he was proclaiming Jesus, that this one is the Son of God. **21** And all who heard were amazed and said, "Is this not the one who in Jerusalem destroyed those who call upon this name, and who had come here for this purpose, that he might bring them bound before the chief priests?" **22** But Saul increased all the more in strength and confounded the Jews dwelling in Damascus, proving that this one is the Christ.

**23** And when a considerable number of days had been fulfilled, the Jews plotted together to kill him. **24** But their plot became known to Saul. And they were also watching the gates day and night, so that they might kill him. **25** But the disciples, taking him by night, let him down through the wall, lowering him in a basket.

**26** And arriving in Jerusalem, he attempted to join himself to the disciples, and they were all afraid of him, not believing that he was a disciple. **27** But Barnabas, taking hold of him, brought him to the apostles, and declared to them how on the road he had seen the Lord, and that he had spoken to him, and how in Damascus he had spoken boldly in the name of Jesus. **28** And he was with them, going in and going out in Jerusalem, speaking boldly in the name of the Lord. **29** And he was speaking and disputing with the Hellenists, but they were attempting to kill him. **30** And when the brothers learned of this, they brought him down to Caesarea and sent him away to Tarsus.

**31** So the church throughout all Judea and Galilee and Samaria had peace, being built up and walking in the fear of the Lord and in the encouragement of the Holy Spirit, and it was multiplied.

**32** And it came to pass that Peter, passing through all the regions, came down also to the saints dwelling in Lydda. **33** And he found there a certain man named Aeneas, who for eight years had been lying on a mat, for he was paralyzed. **34** And Peter said to him, "Aeneas, Jesus the Christ heals you. Rise and make your bed." And immediately he rose. **35** And all those dwelling in Lydda and the Sharon saw him, and they turned to the Lord.

**36** Now in Joppa there was a certain disciple named Tabitha, which translated is called Dorcas. She was full of good works and acts of mercy which she was doing. **37** And it came to pass in those days that she became sick and died. And when they had washed her, they placed her in an upper room. **38** And since Lydda was near to Joppa, the disciples, having heard that Peter was in it, sent two men to him, urging, "Do not delay to come to us." **39** And Peter arose and went with them. And when he arrived, they brought him up to the upper room, and all the widows stood beside him weeping and showing the tunics and garments that Dorcas had made while she was with them. **40** But Peter, having put them all outside and having knelt down, prayed. And turning to the body, he said, "Tabitha, arise." And she opened her eyes, and seeing Peter, she sat up. **41** And giving her his hand, he raised her up. And calling the saints and the widows, he presented her alive. **42** And it became known throughout all Joppa, and many believed in the Lord. **43** And it came to pass that he remained in Joppa for a considerable number of days with a certain Simon, a tanner.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Acts 9 is one of the pivotal chapters in all of Scripture, narrating Saul's encounter with the risen Christ on the Damascus road (vv. 1-19a), his first preaching and escape from Damascus (vv. 19b-25), his reception in Jerusalem through Barnabas (vv. 26-30), a summary of the church's peace and growth (v. 31), and Peter's miracles in Lydda and Joppa -- the healing of Aeneas (vv. 32-35) and the raising of Tabitha (vv. 36-43). The chapter serves as the hinge between the expansion narrative of chapters 6-8 and the Cornelius episode of chapter 10.

**Manuscript condition:** The text begins in the fourth column of page 235 (folio 117v), continues across all four columns of page 236 (folio 118r), and concludes in the first column of page 237 (folio 118v). The manuscript is well-preserved and legible throughout. Standard nomina sacra are used for Lord, Jesus, Christ, God, Spirit. A few corrections by later hands are visible in the Damascus road narrative but do not affect the primary reading.

**Agreement among translators:** The three translations show strong agreement on the narrative structure and core meaning throughout the chapter. The major differences are stylistic: EMET preserves Greek syntax with hyper-literal English; LOGOS maintains formal equivalence with readable English; PNEUMA renders dynamically for contemporary clarity. On substantive textual matters -- the absence of "kick against the goads," the singular "church" in v. 31, the "Son of God" reading in v. 20 -- all three translators agree.

**General translation approach:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS most closely in this chapter, as its formal equivalence best balances fidelity to the Greek with English readability. EMET's contributions are drawn upon where they preserve important Greek features (e.g., "chosen vessel" in v. 15, the genitive absolute in v. 8). PNEUMA's vivid phrasing is occasionally adopted where it captures the force of the Greek without departing from its meaning.

**Key consensus decisions:**

1. **Verses 5-6 -- Absence of "kick against the goads":** All three translators correctly note that Codex Sinaiticus omits the longer reading found in the Textus Receptus: "It is hard for you to kick against the goads" and "Lord, what do you wish me to do?" These expansions were likely harmonized from Acts 26:14 and 22:10 in later manuscripts. Sinaiticus, Vaticanus, and P74 all lack this material. SOPHIA follows the Sinaiticus text, moving directly from "I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting" to "But rise and enter into the city."

2. **Verse 7 -- "hearing the voice":** Sinaiticus reads the accusative for "voice" (ten phonen), meaning the companions heard the sound. This stands in deliberate tension with Acts 22:9, where the genitive is used. SOPHIA preserves this as written, without harmonization.

3. **Verse 8 -- "though his eyes were opened":** The Greek genitive absolute (anewgmenon ton ophthalmon) indicates Saul's eyes were physically open yet he saw nothing -- a theological paradox, not merely closed eyes. LOGOS's rendering "though his eyes were opened" captures this well. PNEUMA's "when he opened his eyes" loses the paradox. EMET's hyper-literal "having-been-opened his eyes" is faithful but awkward. SOPHIA follows LOGOS.

4. **Verse 15 -- "chosen vessel" (skeuos ekloges):** EMET renders "vessel of-election," LOGOS renders "chosen vessel," and PNEUMA renders "chosen instrument." The Greek skeuos means "vessel" or "instrument" -- both are valid. SOPHIA adopts "chosen vessel" (with LOGOS and EMET's sense), as "vessel" preserves the concrete metaphor of the Hebraic genitive of quality and is the more traditional rendering. SOPHIA also retains EMET's "this one is" for the demonstrative houtos, yielding "this one is a chosen vessel to me" rather than LOGOS's "he is a chosen vessel to me" -- preserving the Greek demonstrative.

5. **Verse 17 -- Word order of "the Lord has sent me -- Jesus":** The Greek places "Jesus" in apposition to "the Lord" but after "has sent me," creating a syntactically rough construction. EMET preserves this as "the Lord has-sent me, Jesus." LOGOS uses an em-dash: "the Lord has sent me -- Jesus, who appeared to you." PNEUMA renders "the Lord Jesus ... has sent me," smoothing the syntax entirely. SOPHIA follows LOGOS's em-dash approach, which preserves the roughness while remaining readable, but uses the double-dash convention consistent with prior SOPHIA chapters.

6. **Verse 18 -- "something like scales" (hos lepides):** All three translators preserve the comparative particle hos ("as/like"), indicating Luke describes an appearance, not a medical diagnosis. SOPHIA renders "something like scales" with LOGOS and PNEUMA.

7. **Verse 20 -- "Son of God":** Sinaiticus reads ton huion tou theou ("the Son of God"), confirmed by P74 and Vaticanus. Some Western witnesses read "the Christ" here. All three translators correctly follow Sinaiticus.

8. **Verse 22 -- "the Christ" vs. "the Messiah":** EMET and LOGOS render ho Christos as "the Christ"; PNEUMA renders "the Messiah." SOPHIA retains "the Christ" for consistency with the established convention throughout the New Testament translation. The synagogue context where "Messiah" might carry more force does not override the need for terminological consistency.

9. **Verse 25 -- "the disciples" vs. "his disciples":** Sinaiticus reads hoi mathetai ("the disciples") without the pronoun autou ("his"). EMET and LOGOS both render "the/his disciples." The manuscript image on page 236 confirms no autou. SOPHIA renders "the disciples" following the Sinaiticus reading precisely. LOGOS's rendering "his disciples" adds a pronoun not in this manuscript.

10. **Verse 31 -- Singular "church" (he ekklesia):** All three translators correctly follow Sinaiticus's singular reading. This is the harder reading (the plural "churches" in later manuscripts is an obvious smoothing) and presents the early community as one unified body across three regions. SOPHIA renders "the church" with singular verbs.

11. **Verse 34 -- "Jesus the Christ" vs. "Jesus Christ":** LOGOS renders "Jesus the Christ"; EMET has "Jesus Christ"; PNEUMA has "Jesus the Messiah." The Sinaiticus text reads Iesous ho Christos, with the article -- making "the Christ" a title rather than a name component. SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "Jesus the Christ."

12. **Verse 36 -- "acts of mercy" vs. "acts of charity":** EMET renders eleemosynon as "acts-of-mercy," LOGOS as "acts of charity," PNEUMA as "helping the poor." The Greek encompasses both mercy and almsgiving. SOPHIA renders "acts of mercy" -- eleemosyne is rooted in eleos (mercy), and "mercy" is the more faithful rendering, while "charity" has shifted in English to mean primarily financial giving.

13. **Verse 36 -- Tabitha/Dorcas:** All three translators preserve Luke's bilingual note, explaining the Aramaic name Tabitha with its Greek equivalent Dorcas ("Gazelle"). SOPHIA preserves both names and Luke's explanatory gloss as found in Sinaiticus.

14. **Verse 43 -- "tanner" vs. "leatherworker":** EMET and LOGOS render burseus as "tanner"; PNEUMA renders "leatherworker." SOPHIA retains "tanner" as the standard and more precise translation. PNEUMA's note about the ritual impurity implications is theologically astute but does not require a different translation of the word itself.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | "breathing threat and murder" phrasing | "breathing threat and murder" | "breathing threat and murder" | "breathing out murderous threats" | "breathing threat and murder" -- 2-to-1; preserves the two genitives (threat AND murder) as separate nouns |
| 1 | Opening conjunction | "But Saul" | "But Saul" | "Meanwhile, Saul" | "But Saul" -- Greek de is adversative here, not merely transitional |
| 1 | "went to" vs. "having approached" | "having approached" | "went to" | "went to" | "went to" -- 2-to-1; the aorist participle proselthon functions as a finite verb in narrative |
| 2 | "of the Way" phrasing | "being of the Way" | "who were of the Way" | "belonged to the Way" | "who were of the Way" -- follows LOGOS; all three correctly capitalize "Way" as a proper title |
| 5-6 | "Kick against the goads" | Omitted (correctly) | Omitted (correctly) | Omitted (correctly) | Omitted -- Sinaiticus lacks this Textus Receptus interpolation; all three agree |
| 7 | "voice" or "sound" | "the voice" | "the voice" | "the sound of the voice" | "the voice" -- Greek phonen (accusative); PNEUMA adds "sound of" not in the text |
| 8 | Eyes opened paradox | "having-been-opened his eyes, he was-seeing nothing" | "though his eyes were opened, he saw nothing" | "when he opened his eyes, he could not see a thing" | Follows LOGOS; the genitive absolute conveys eyes open yet blind -- PNEUMA's "when he opened" implies volition not in the Greek |
| 10 | "disciple" vs. "follower" | "disciple" | "disciple" | "follower" | "disciple" -- Greek mathetes; SOPHIA retains the traditional rendering throughout |
| 12 | "in a vision" included | Yes (en horamati) | Yes | Yes (integrated) | Yes -- Sinaiticus includes "in a vision" describing Saul's seeing of Ananias |
| 15 | "chosen vessel" vs. "chosen instrument" | "vessel of-election" | "chosen vessel" | "chosen instrument" | "chosen vessel" -- skeuos means vessel/container; preserves the Hebraic genitive metaphor |
| 15 | "nations" vs. "Gentiles" | "nations" | "Gentiles" | "nations" | "Gentiles" -- ethnon in this context specifically means non-Jewish peoples, which "Gentiles" conveys |
| 17 | Syntax of "Lord ... Jesus" apposition | "the Lord has-sent me, Jesus, the one who-appeared" | "the Lord has sent me -- Jesus, who appeared" | "the Lord Jesus ... has sent me" | Double-dash approach: "the Lord has sent me -- Jesus, who appeared" -- preserves the grammatical roughness without PNEUMA's smoothing |
| 18 | "scales" simile | "as scales" | "something like scales" | "something like scales" | "something like scales" -- 2-to-1; hos lepides rendered with the comparative particle preserved |
| 22 | "the Christ" vs. "the Messiah" | "the Christ" | "the Christ" | "the Messiah" | "the Christ" -- 2-to-1; maintains consistency across the translation |
| 25 | "the disciples" vs. "his disciples" | "the disciples" | "his disciples" | "his followers" | "the disciples" -- Sinaiticus lacks autou; SOPHIA follows the manuscript precisely |
| 25 | "basket" type | "basket" (spuris) | "basket" | "large basket" | "basket" -- PNEUMA adds "large" as interpretation; the Greek spuris is simply "basket" (its size is implied but unstated here) |
| 29 | "Hellenists" vs. "Greek-speaking Jews" | "Hellenists" | "Hellenists" | "Greek-speaking Jews" | "Hellenists" -- 2-to-1; the technical term is well-established and preserves the Greek Hellenistas |
| 31 | "walking in" vs. "living in" | "going in the fear" | "walking in the fear" | "living in reverent awe" | "walking in the fear" -- Greek poreuomene means "walking/going," a metaphor for conduct |
| 33 | Duration phrasing | "from eight years" | "for eight years" | "for eight years" | "for eight years" -- 2-to-1; English idiom; EMET's "from" is hyper-literal of Greek ex |
| 34 | "Jesus the Christ" vs. "Jesus Christ" | "Jesus Christ" | "Jesus the Christ" | "Jesus the Messiah" | "Jesus the Christ" -- Sinaiticus has the article (ho Christos), making it a title |
| 36 | "acts of mercy" vs. "acts of charity" | "acts-of-mercy" | "acts of charity" | "helping the poor" | "acts of mercy" -- eleemosyne derives from eleos (mercy); more faithful than "charity" |
| 38 | "come to us" phrasing | "come-through to us" | "come to us" | "come to us without delay" | "come to us" -- PNEUMA moves "do not delay" into the same clause; SOPHIA keeps the structure of LOGOS |
| 40 | "Tabitha, arise" vs. "get up" | "Tabitha, rise-up" | "Tabitha, arise" | "Tabitha, get up" | "Tabitha, arise" -- the solemn register befits a resurrection command; echoes "Talitha koum" (Mark 5:41) |
| 43 | "tanner" vs. "leatherworker" | "tanner" | "tanner" | "leatherworker" | "tanner" -- 2-to-1; standard translation of burseus; PNEUMA's modernization is unnecessary |

-- SOPHIA
